segments.title: Core reference books for Blackburn aircraft
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke points to aircraft-specification and Blackburn history books as starting references for tracing the company's designs.
segments.body: This short guide identifies the printed works behind the Blackburn series, including a British aircraft-specification reference and A. J. Jackson's Blackburn Aircraft since 1909. Dr. Clarke shows a former Aberdeenshire library copy of Jackson's company history and notes that Putnam published it in 1968. The pairing matters because the specification volume supplies the formal requirements, while the Blackburn history follows the manufacturer's aircraft across time. Together they offer a practical starting point for tracing individual designs and placing them within the company's wider interwar naval-aviation work.

evidence.note: The transcript names a British aircraft-specification volume and A. J. Jackson's Blackburn Aircraft since 1909 as useful sources.

segments.title: Why Blackburn's period aircraft names need checking
segments.summary: A history website helps reconcile older Blackburn aircraft names and variant labels with the names used by modern institutions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends the British Aerospace history website when a historic aircraft name does not match the modern name a reader encounters elsewhere. He explains that period names, later dominant labels, and variant names can pull in different directions, with a label once used for a variant sometimes becoming the aircraft's better-known name. His preference for period terminology comes from doctoral research on aircraft carriers and naval aviation in the 1920s and 1930s. That research treated carrier and aircraft development as connected processes, so identifying each design consistently is necessary when following how the two evolved alongside one another. The passage is therefore a compact lesson in source criticism as well as a reading recommendation.

evidence.note: The transcript recommends a British Aerospace history site and explains the mismatch between period aircraft names, variants, and later naming.
